verses.text: Jehojada a conclu un pacte avec des officiers militaires de premier plan, qui ont rassemblé un large soutien populaire parmi les Lévites et les chefs de clan. Le contenu de ce pacte, établi autour de Joas, apparaît dans la déclaration de Jehojada affirmant que le fils du roi devait monter sur le trône.

Jehojada a conclu un pacte avec des officiers militaires de premier plan, qui ont rassemblé un large soutien populaire parmi les Lévites et les chefs de clan. Le contenu de ce pacte, établi autour de Joas, apparaît dans la déclaration de Jehojada affirmant que le fils du roi devait monter sur le trône.

Jehojada a conclu un pacte avec des officiers militaires de premier plan, qui ont rassemblé un large soutien populaire parmi les Lévites et les chefs de clan. Le contenu de ce pacte, établi autour de Joas, apparaît dans la déclaration de Jehojada affirmant que le fils du roi devait monter sur le trône.

Le moment le plus stratégique pour le coup d'État se situait lors du changement de la garde du temple, au milieu des nombreux mouvements de personnes. Un tiers des gardes prenait son service, réparti entre trois postes pour assurer les tâches régulières et surveiller toute activité venant du palais. Le reste de la force armée se tenait dans les parvis du temple de l'Éternel, formant un mur humain pour protéger le roi.

Le moment le plus stratégique pour le coup d'État se situait lors du changement de la garde du temple, au milieu des nombreux mouvements de personnes. Un tiers des gardes prenait son service, réparti entre trois postes pour assurer les tâches régulières et surveiller toute activité venant du palais. Le reste de la force armée se tenait dans les parvis du temple de l'Éternel, formant un mur humain pour protéger le roi.
Puisque seuls les sacrificateurs et les Lévites en service étaient autorisés à entrer dans le temple, les Lévites devaient empêcher toute autre personne d'y accéder.
Puisque seuls les sacrificateurs et les Lévites en service étaient autorisés à entrer dans le temple, les Lévites devaient empêcher toute autre personne d'y accéder.

23:8 Jehoiada . . . dismissed not the courses—As it was necessary to have as large a disposable force as he could command on such a crisis, the high priest detained those who, in other circumstances, would have returned home on the expiry of their week of service.

23:9 Jehoiada the priest delivered to the captains of hundreds spears, and bucklers: The priestly armory included weapons of David (cp. 2 Sam 8:7-12), preserved in the temple as votive offerings. Jehoiada distributes them for the coup that crowns the boy Joash — a moment when sacred space yielded its dedicated arms to protect the rightful Davidic heir.

23:10 He set all the people, every man having his weapon in his hand: Jehoiada's coronation of the boy-king Joash is protected by armed Levites stationed around the temple. The unusual arming of the worshipers reflects the fragility of restoration — the rightful Davidic line had to be defended even within the house of the Lord (cp. 23:6-9).
Lorsque Joas, âgé de sept ans, a été oint et couronné roi, il a reçu une copie de la loi, conformément aux prescriptions de la loi (voir **Dt 17.18-20** ).

23:12 ATHALIAH SLAIN. (2 Chr 23:12-15) Athaliah heard the noise of the people—The unusual commotion, indicated by the blast of the trumpets and the vehement acclamations of the people, drew her attention, or excited her fears. She might have flattered herself that, having slain all the royal family, she was in perfect security; but it is just as likely that, finding on reflection, one had escaped her murderous hands, she might not deem it expedient to institute any enquiries; but the very idea would keep her constantly in a state of jealous suspicion and irritation.

23:13 behold, the king stood at his pillar at the entering in—The king's pillar was in the people's court, opposite that of the priests'. The young king, arrayed in the royal insignia, had been brought out of the inner, to stand forth in the outer court, to the public view. Some think that he stood on the brazen scaffold of Solomon, erected beside the pillar [see on 2 Chr 6:13].

23:14 Slay her not in the house of the Lord . . . and when she was come to the entering of the horse gate by the king's house, they slew her there—The high priest ordered her immediately to be taken out of the temple grounds and put to death. "And they laid hands on her; and she went by the way by the which horses came into the king's house: and there was she slain" (Kg2 11:16). "Now, we are not to suppose that horses came into [the king's house] of residence, but into the king's (horses') house or hippodrome (the gate of the king's mules) [JOSEPHUS], he had built for them on the southeast of the temple, in the immediate vicinity of the horse gate in the valley of…

23:15 They laid hands on her, and when she was come to the entering of the horse gate: Athaliah's execution is removed from the temple precincts to honor the holiness of the house (cp. 23:14). *Sha'ar hassusim* — the horse gate — opened onto the royal stables east of the temple, an appropriate civil location for the queen-usurper's death.

Le pacte comprenait des vœux entre le roi et le peuple, entre l'Éternel et le roi, ainsi qu'entre l'Éternel et le peuple. Les engagements pris envers l'Éternel incluaient l'élimination du culte de Baal.

Le pacte comprenait des vœux entre le roi et le peuple, entre l'Éternel et le roi, ainsi qu'entre l'Éternel et le peuple. Les engagements pris envers l'Éternel incluaient l'élimination du culte de Baal.
En organisant l'accession de Joas sur le trône, le sacrificateur Jehojada a restauré la dynastie de David et rétabli le culte du temple, conformément aux instructions établies par David.
En organisant l'accession de Joas sur le trône, le sacrificateur Jehojada a restauré la dynastie de David et rétabli le culte du temple, conformément aux instructions établies par David.

23:20 He took the captains of hundreds, and the nobles: The procession from temple to palace formally installs Joash. The order — through the high gate into the king's house — re-establishes the divinely ordained pattern: priesthood escorts the Davidic king from his consecration to his throne (cp. 1 Kgs 1:38-40).

23:21 The city was quiet - There was no attempt at a counter-revolution. Concerning the coronation of Joash, there is a curious circumstance mentioned by the Targumist on 2 Chr 23:11, it is as follows: - "And they brought forth the son of the king, and put on him the royal crown which David took from the head of the king of the children of Ammon. In it was inserted the precious attracting stone, in which was engraven and expressed the great and honorable Name [יהוה] which David had placed there by the Holy Spirit: and it was of the weight of a talent of gold;
